Example: \(\geq\) is a relation on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\) such that \(x\geq y\) iff \(x\) is greater than or equal to \(y\) \(>\) is a relation on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\) such that \(x > y\) iff \(x\) is strictly greater than \(y\) We can view \(\geq\) as a subset of \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\). In notation: \(\{(x,y) \in \mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}: x \geq y\}\) A relation, \(R\), on \(X 	imes Y\) is some \(R \subseteq X 	imes Y, (x,y) \in R\). Can also write \(xRy\). Possible properties of a relation, \(R\), on \(X 	imes X\): Reflexive: for each \(x \in X, xRx\) Transitive: if \(xRy\) and \(yRz\) then \(xRz\). Symmetric: if \(xRy\) then \(yRx\) Antisymmetric: if \(xRy\) and \(yRx\) then \(x=y\). Complete: for each \(x, y \in X\), either \(xRy\) or \(yRx\). Example: \(\geq\) is reflexive transitive NOT symmetric antisymmetric (only way for to be greater than y and y greater than x is for them to be equal) complete (yep, can order any two numbers) Example: \(>\) is NOT reflexive transitive NOT symmetric YES antisymmetric (trivially - the sufficient condition is not satisfied (impossible for \(xRy\) and \(yRx\) to happen here), so the necessary condition is irrelevant) NOT complete (can’t order the same number) Example: \(\geq\) on \(\mathbb{R} 	imes \mathbb{R}\), such that \((x_1,x_2) \geq (y_1,y_2) \iff x_1 \geq y_1, x_2 \geq y_2\). This is reflexive transitive NOT symmetric antisymmetric NOT complete (cannot rank ordered pairs) 1.1.1 Def “Equivalence Relation” An Equivalence Relation on \(X 	imes X\) is 1. reflexive 2. symmetric 3. transitive Example: \(X = \{a,c,l\}\). Preference relationship on \(X 	imes X: R\) is \(aRl, lRA, aRA, lRl, cRc, aRc, lRc\). This is not an equivalence relation because it is not symmetric. But, I can derive an indifference relationship, \(I\) from \(R\): a\(I\)l, l\(I\)a, a\(I\)a, l\(I\)l, c\(I\)c. And this is an equivalence relation!

3 Real Numbers 3.0.1 Def “bounds” Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. Call \(u \in \mathbb{R}\) an upper bound of \(X\) if, for each \(x \in X, u \geq x\) Call \(l \in \mathbb{R}\) a lower bound of \(X\) if, for each \(x \in X, x \geq l\) 3.0.2 Def “supremum” Call \(u^*\) a least upper bound (supremum) of \(X\) if \(u^*\) is an upper bound of \(X\) and, for each upper bound \(u, u \geq u^*\). Denoted \(\sup X\). Remark \(\sup X\) and \(\inf X\) need not be members of \(X\). Proof: Consider \([0,1) = X\). Then \(sup X = 1\). And \(1 \geq X\) for \(x \in X\). \(1 > u\) there exists \(1 > x > u\) and so \(u\) is not an upper bound. 3.0.3 Completeness Axiom: Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) be nonempty. If \(X\) is bounded above (resp bounded below) then \(X\) has a least upper bound (resp a greatest lower bound.) Notational convention: If \(X\) nonempty and has an upper bound, then \(\sup X \in \mathbb{R}\) exists. But if \(X\) nonempty and does not have an upper bound, we write \(\sup X = \infty\). 3.0.4 Prop 1: “sup is unique” Suppose \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is nonempty and has an upper bound. Then \(\sup X\) is unique for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(x \in X\) such that \(\sup X - \varepsilon < x\). Proof fix \(u_1, u_2\) that are both least upper bounds of \(X\). Then \(u_1 \leq u_2\), since \(u_1\) is a least upper bound. Similarly, \(u_2 \leq u_1\), since \(u_2\) is a least upper bound. Hence, \(u_1=u_2\). By contradiction. Suppose not. Then there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that for all \(x \in X, \sup X - \varepsilon \geq x\). Then \(\sup X - \varepsilon\) is an upper bound with \(\sup X - \varepsilon < \sup X\) contradicting \(\sup X\) being supremum. 3.0.5 Def “max/min” Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty Call \(\bar{x}\) a max of \(X\) if \(\bar{x} \in X\) and \(\bar{x} \geq x\) for each \(x \in X\) Call \(\underline{x}\) a min of \(X\) if \(\underline{x} \in X\) and \(\underline{x} \leq x\) for each \(x \in X\) Example: [0,1) doesn’t have a max, even though it’s bounded 3.0.6 Prop 2 “max=sup” Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty \(\bar{x}\) is a max of \(X\) iff \(\bar{x}=\sup X \in X\) \(\underline{x}\) is a max of \(X\) iff \(\underline{x}=\inf X \in X\) Proof: a) \(\bar{x} \in X\), we know this from the def of the max. b) need to show \(\bar{x}=\sup X\). Well, we know \(\bar{x}\) is an upper bound and there exists a least upper bound \(=sup X\) by the completeness axiom. Since \(\bar{x} \in X\) each upper bound of \(X\) is greater than or equal to \(\bar{x}\). Hence, \(\bar{x}=\sup X\). 3.0.7 Corollary 1: Fix \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. Then \(X\) has a max iff \(\sup X \in X\). Moreover, if \(X\) has a max, it is unique. A real-valued function, \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\). Call \(f\) bounded if \(f(X) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is bounded.

4 Metric Spaces 4.0.1 Def “metric” A metric on \(X\) is a function \(d: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y) \geq 0\) for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y) = 0\) iff \(x=y\) for each \(x,y\in X, d(x,y)=d(y,x)\) (symmetry) for each \(x,y,z\in X, d(x,z) \leq d(x,y) + d(y,z)\) (triangle inequality) 4.0.2 Def “metric space” A metric space is a pair \((X,d)\) where \(X\) is a nonempty set and \(d\) is a metric on \(X\). Example 1: Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) nonempty. The usual metric is \(d^u: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d^u(x,y) = |x-y|\). Example 2: \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) nonempty. The euclidean metric \(d^{euc}: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is \(d^{euc}(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sqrt{\Sigma^n_{i=1} (x_i-y_i)^2}\) Note that when \(n=1\) we have \(d^u(x,y)=d^{euc}(x,y)\) Any time we look at a subset of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) we’re going to impose euclidean distance, unless we are explicitly told otherwise. Example 3: “taxicab” metric. \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) nonempty: \(d^t(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^n |x_i - y_i|\) \(d^t(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) \geq d^{euc}(\vec{x}, \vec{y})\). Example 4: Fix some nonempty set, \(X\). Discrete metric: \(d^{disc} (x,y) = \{1 \text{ if } x \neq y, 0 \text{ if } x=y\) Example 5: “uniform metric” \((X, \rho), (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\), where \(\rho: X\times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is a metric. \(F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) =\) set of bounded \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) \(d: F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) \times F^B(X, \mathbb{R}) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d(f,g) = \sup_{x\in X} d^u(f(x), g(x)) = \sup_{x\in X} |f(x) - g(x)|\)

5 Sequences 5.0.1 Def “sequence” Fix a metric space \((X,d)\). A sequence on \((X,d)\) is a function \(f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow X\). 5.0.2 Def “limit” Fix a sequence \((x_n:n\in \mathbb{N})\) on \((X,d)\). The limit of \((x_n:n\in \mathbb{N})\) is some \(x \in X\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) with \(d(x_n, x)< \varepsilon\) for all \(n\geq N\). The sequence converges if it has a limit in \(X\). The sequence diverges if it does not have a limit in \(X\). 5.0.3 Prop 1 “sequences have max one limit” Any sequence has at most one limit. Proof: Fix a sequence \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) and suppose that \(x, y\) are both limits of the sequence. want to show: x = y We know there exists \(N(x)\) such that for all \(n \geq N(x), d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon / 2\) there exists \(N(y)\) such that for all \(n \geq N(y), d(x_n, y) < \varepsilon / 2\) Let \(N = \max\{N(x), N(y)\}\). For each \(n \geq N\), \(d(x,y) \geq d(x, x_n) + d(x_n,y) < \varepsilon /2 + \varepsilon / 2 = \varepsilon\). So, \(d(x,y) < \varepsilon\) for all \(\varepsilon > 0\). So, \(d(x,y)=0\). So, \(x=y\). QED. 5.0.4 Def “Limit” A limit of \((x_n, n\in \mathbb{N})\) is some \(x \in X\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\), there exists \(N\) with \(d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \geq N\). The sequence converges if it has a limit in \(X\). Otherwise it diverges. Special case of interest: real-valued sequence (whenever \(X\) is a subset of the reals), \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}, d = d^u\) A sequence is just a mapping of the natural numbers to \(X\). (i.e., \(f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow X\)) Example 1: \((1/n, n \geq 1)\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\). \(\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} 1/n = 0\): Fix \(\varepsilon >0\). To show: there exists \(N\) such that \[|1/n - 0| < \varepsilon\] for all \(n \geq N\). Take \(N = 1/\varepsilon\). Rearrange to get \(1/N < \varepsilon\). And clearly, any \(n > N\) will also satisfy this inequality. So, we have proved that the limit converges to 0. Example 1b \((1/n, n\geq 2)\) on \(((0,1), d^u)\) This diverges to 0. It gets closer to 0, but the limit is not in our set, so it’s divergent. Example 2 \((n: n\geq 1)\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) Can there exist some \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(N\) where \(|n-x| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \geq N\)? No. For each \(\varepsilon\) there is some \(N\) with \(N > x + \varepsilon\). I also know for every \(n \geq N, |n - x| > \varepsilon\). 5.0.5 Def “divergence” Fix a real-valued sequence, \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) on \((X, d^u)\). \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) diverges to \(\infty\) if, for each \(k\in \mathbb{R}\), there exists \(N\) such that \(x_n>k\) for all \(n\geq N\). \((x_n: n\geq 1)\) diverges to \(-\infty\) if, for each \(k\in \mathbb{R}\), there exists \(N\) such that \(x_n<k\) for all \(n\geq N\). It’s convenient to write \(lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} x_n = \infty\). But this is an abuse of notation. Just remember that it doesn’t actually converge (bc you can’t converge outside your set). Case \((\mathbb{R}^m, d^{euc})\), \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) where \(x_n \in \mathbb{R}^m\). So \(x_n = (x_{n1}, x_{n2}, ..., x_{nm}) \in \mathbb{R}^m\) Result the sequence \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x^*\in\mathbb{R}^m\) iff for each \(i=1, ..., m\), \((x_{ni}: n\in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x_i^*\) in \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) Case \((X, d^{disc})\) the seq \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\in X\) iff there exists some \(n\geq N\) such that \(x_n=x\) for all \(n\geq N\). Suppose \((x_n: n \in \mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\in X\). Then, there exists some \(N\) such that \(1/2 > d^{disc}(x_n,x) \Rightarrow x_n=x\) for all \(n \geq N\) since \(d^{disc}(x_n,x) \in \{0,1\}\) (note that 1/2 is arbitrary–any value between 0 and 1 would have worked here.) 5.1 Subsequences seq \(= (1,6, 2, 9, 7, 5, 22, 1, ...)\) subsequence \(= (1,6, 9, 5, 22, ...)\) 5.1.1 Def “subsequence” Fix a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\). Call \((y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) a subsequence if there exists a function \(f: \mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) such that for each \(k\in \mathbb{N}\), \(f(k+1) > f(k)\geq k\), \(y_k=x_{f(k)}\) 5.1.2 Prop 2 “sequence converges \(\Rightarrow\) subsequences converge” Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a sequence on \((X,d)\) that converges to \(x\). Then each subsequence of \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) also converges to \(x\). Proof Let \((y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a subsequence of \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) associated with a function \(f: \mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}\). Since \((x_n: n\in\mathbb{N})\) converges to \(x\) for each \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon\), for all \(n \geq N\). There exists \(K\) such that \(f(K) \geq N\). Then it follows \(d(y_k, x) = d(x_{f(k)}, x) < \varepsilon\) for all \(k \geq K\) since \(f(k) \geq N\). Example 3 \(((-1)^n: n\geq 1)\). Pick two subsequences: \((-1, -1,..., ) \rightarrow\) converges to -1 \((1, 1,..., ) \rightarrow\) converges to 1 but a sequence cannot have two limits, so this sequence must diverge. 5.1.3 Def “bounded sequence” Call a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) bounded if there exists \(y\in X\) and \(M>0\) such that \(d(x_n, y) \leq M\) for all \(n\in \mathbb{N}\). 5.1.4 Prop 3 “A convergent sequence is bounded.” Proof Omitted Caution: not every bounded sequence is convergent (example: \((-1)^n\) on \(X =\mathbb{R}, d^u\)) 5.1.5 Def “increasing” Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be a real-valued sequence. The sequence is increasing if for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}, x_{n+1} \geq x_n\). 5.1.6 Def “monotone” The sequence is monotone if it is either increasing or decreasing. 5.1.7 Prop 4 “bounded, monotone \(\Rightarrow\) convergent” If \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) is a bounded, monotone sequence on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) then it is convergent. Proof Fix \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) that is bounded, monotone sequence. Without loss of generality, assume it is increasing. Since bounded, the set \(\{x_n: n\in\mathbb{N}\}\) has to be bounded above (note: switching to set so we can use completeness axiom. apparently that may not apply to sequences, but it does to sets). By completeness axiom, if there is an upper bound, there must be a least upper bound, \(x\in \mathbb{R}\). Want to show: \(x=\lim x_n\) To show: Fix \(\varepsilon >0\), Find some \(N\) such that \(|x_n-x| < \varepsilon\), for all \(n \geq N\). Note: there exists \(N\) such that \(x_N > x-\varepsilon\), by Prop 1 of the real numbers notes. Since the seq is increasing \(x_n \geq x_N \geq x-\varepsilon\). Rearrange to get \(\varepsilon > x-x_n\). Since \(x\) is an upper bound (the supremum) we have \(x > x_n\) which means \(x-x_n = |x-x_n|\). So, we have \(\varepsilon > |x-x_n|\). QED. 5.1.8 Prop 5 “squeeze thm” Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (y_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (z_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be sequences on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\). If, for each \(n\), \(x_n \leq y_n \leq z_n\) and \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} z_n = a\) then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n = a\) Note: even if \(x_n\) and \(z_n\) crossed over each other at some points, I might still be able to use the squeeze thm by using subsequences to satisfy the fact that for each \(n\), \(x_n \leq y_n \leq z_n\) 5.1.9 Prop 6 “limit algebra” Let \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N}), (y_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) be sequences on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) with \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n = x, \lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n = y\). Then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} |x_n| = |x|\) \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_n + y_n = x+y\) \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} x_ny_n = xy\) If \(x_n \neq 0 \forall n\) and \(x\neq 0\), then \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty} y_n/x_n= y/x\) 5.1.10 Prop 7: Bolzano-Weierstrauss Thm “bounded seq \(\Rightarrow\) convergent subsequence” Every bounded sequence on \((\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) has a convergent subsequence. 5.1.11 Def “Cauchy” Call a sequence Cauchy if for each \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) such that for all \(n, m \geq N, d(x_n, x_m) < \varepsilon\). 5.1.12 Prop 8 “convergent \(\Rightarrow\) Cauchy” Any convergent sequence is Cauchy. Proof Fix a sequence \((x_n: n\in \mathbb{N})\) with \(\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}x_n=x\). So there exists \(g: (0,\infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) such that for each \(\varepsilon >0\) and \(n\geq g(\varepsilon), d(x_n,x) < \varepsilon\). To show it’s Cauchy, fix \(\varepsilon >0\) and choose \(N = g(\varepsilon/2)\). By triangle inequality I know \(d(x_n,x_m) \leq d(x_n, x) + d(x, x_m) < \varepsilon/2 + \varepsilon/2 = \varepsilon\). Remark: a seq can be Cauchy without converging. Consider example 1b: \((1/n, n\geq 2)\) on \(((0,1), d^u)\). This doesn’t converge (because 1 isn’t in the metric space), but it is cauchy. (i.e., for \(\varepsilon >0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(|1/n -1/m| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n, m \geq N\).) 5.1.13 Def complete space A metric space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges. Examples of complete metric spaces: \((\mathbb{R}^n, d^{euc})\) \((X, d^{disc})\) for \(X\) finite \(((0,1), d^u)\) is NOT complete \((\mathbb{Q}, d^u)\) is NOT complete Remark: All Cauchy sequences converge in complete spaces. 5.1.14 Prop 9 “Cauchy \(\Rightarrow\) bounded” Every Cauchy sequence is bounded.

6 Open/closed Sets 6.0.0.1 Class 4: 2023-08-17 Example 0: \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) 6.0.1 Def “open ball” \(E \subseteq X\) is an open ball around \(x \in X\) if there exists \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x, \varepsilon) = \{y \in X: d(x,y) < \varepsilon\} = E\) Example 1a \((X,d)=(\mathbb{R}, d^u)\) \(B(x,\varepsilon) = (x-\varepsilon, x + \varepsilon)\) Example 1b \((X,d)=([0,10], d^u)\) \(B(1/2, 3/4) = [0,5/4)\) (adding and subtracting epsilon of 3/4 from 1/2, but since -1/4 is outside our interval, just get as close as you can with 0, closed. still an open ball, just because it’s in [0,10]. but this would not be an open ball in \(\mathbb{R}\)) Example 2 \((X,d) = (\mathbb{R}^2, d^{euc})\) 6.0.2 Def “interior point” Fix \(E \subseteq X\). Say \(x\) is an interior point of \(E\) if there exists some \(\varepsilon >0\) such that \(B(x,\varepsilon) \subseteq E\). \(int(E) = \{x \in X: x 	ext{ is an interior point of } E\}\). If \(x \in B(x,\varepsilon)\subseteq E)\) then \(int(E) \subseteq E\). 6.0.3 Def “open set” A set \(E \subseteq X\) is an open set if for each \(x \in E, x \in int(E)\) \(E \subseteq int(E) \subseteq E\) - so an open set has \(E = int(E).\) Whether a set is an open set depends on \((X,d)\). That is, if \(\{F_i: i \in I\}\) is an open cover of \(E\) then there exists finite \(J \subseteq I\) such that \(\{F_i: i \in J\}\) is also a cover of \(E\). 7 Continuity Fix \((X, d_x)\) and \((Y,d_y)\). Look at \(f: X \rightarrow Y\). 7.0.1 Def “continuous at” Say a function \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is continuous at \(x\) if for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta >0\) such that \(d_x(x,x') < \delta\) implies \(d_y(f(x),f(x')) < \varepsilon\) for each \(\delta > 0\) there is an \(x'\) with \(d_x(x,x') < \delta\) but \(f(x') > J\) and so \(d_y(f(x),f(x')) > \varepsilon\) \(\varepsilon = d_y(f(x), J)\) 7.0.2 Prop 1 “continuous \(\iff\) limits contained” A function \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is continuous at \(x\in X \iff\) for each sequence \((x_n: n \geq 1)\) on \((X, d_x)\) with \(lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} x_n = x \in X, lim_{n\rightarrow \infty} f(x_n) = f(x) \in Y\) 7.0.3 Def “continuous function” A function \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is continuous if it is continuous at each \(x\in X\). 7.0.4 Prop 2 “continuous \(\iff\) inverse is open” A function \(f: X\rightarrow X\) is continuous if and only if, for each open set, \(U \subseteq Y, f^{-1}(U) \subseteq X\) is open. \(x \in f^{-1}(u)\) is not an interior point because for each \(B(x, \varepsilon)\) there is discontinuity within the ball. Example 1 Simple examples of continuous functions: \((X, d_x) = (Y,d_y)\), take \(f: X\rightarrow Y\) to be the identity function, \(f(x)=x\in Y = X\). Fix \(U \subseteq Y = X\) open in \((Y, d_y) \Rightarrow f^{-1}(U)=U\) open in \((X,d_x)=(Y,d_y)\) start with \(\{x\}\) in \((Y,d_y) = (\mathbb{R}, d^{disc})\) then \(f^{-1}(\{x\}) = \{x\}\) not open in \((\mathbb{R}, d^{u})\) singleton sets are open in discrete metric, but not in Euclidean metric! Constant function: \(f: X\rightarrow Y\) there exists \(y^* \in Y\) such that \(f(x)=y^*\) for all \(x\in X\). Example 2 \((X, d_x)\) where \(X\) is finite. Any \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is continuous. key: since \(X\) is finite, \(f^{-1}(U)\) must be open since any subset of a finite set is open. Example 3 \(X=Y=\mathbb{R}, d_x=d_y=d^u\). \(f(x)=mx+b\) for \(m \neq 0\). to show \(f\) is continuous, fix \(\varepsilon >0\) and some \(\delta >0\) such that \(|x-x'|< \delta\) then \(|mx+b - (mx' + b)| = |m||x-x'| < \varepsilon\). Take \(\delta = \varepsilon / |m|\) and we can show this. 7.0.5 Prop 3 “combining continuous functions” Fix \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) and \(g: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) that are both continuous (on \((x, d_x), (\mathbb{R}, d^u)\)) \(f+g\) is continuous (\(x \rightarrow f(x) + g(x)\)) \(f * g\) is continuous (\(x \rightarrow f(x) * g(x)\)) \(f/g\) is continuous (if, for each \(x\in X, g(x)\neq 0\)) (\(x \rightarrow f(x) / g(x)\)) \(\max\{f,g\}\) is continuous (\(x \rightarrow \max\{f(x), g(x)\}\)) \(\min\{f,g\}\) is continuous (\(x \rightarrow \min\{f(x), g(x)\}\)) \(|f|\) is continuous (\(x\rightarrow |f(x)|\)) so, using prop 3 on example 3 could simplify things. we’d just say, “well, we know any constant (\(m\) and \(b\)) function is continuous, and we know the identity function (\(f(x) = x\)) is continuous, and we know that the product of two continuous functions is continuous (number 2 in prop 3) and the sum of two continuous function (\(mx\) and \(b\)) is continuous, so \(f(x) = mx + b\) is continuous.” 7.1 Other notions of continuity 7.1.1 Def “lower semi-continuous” Fix a function \(f: X\rightarrow \mathbb{R}\). \(f\) is lower semi-continuous if, for each \(c \in \mathbb{R}, f^{-1}(c,\infty)\) is open \(f\) is upper semi-continuous if, for each \(c \in \mathbb{R}, f^{-1}(-\infty, c)\) is open It’s immediate that if \(f\) is continuous, then it’s both upper and lower semi-continuous. It’s less obvious (though we will see this is, indeed, true later) that if \(f\) if upper and lower semi-continuous that \(f\) is continuous. Example 4b \(f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) such that \[f(x) = \begin{cases} 0 \text{ if } x \leq 0 \\ 1 \text{ if } x>0 \end{cases}\] Choose \(c = 1/2\), an arbitrary point between 0 and 1. Checking upper semi-continuity: \[f^{-1}(-\infty, 1/2) = (-\infty, 0]\] this is not open, so we fail upper semi-continuity. so, \(f\) is not continuous. Checking lower semi-continuity: \[f^{-1}(c,\infty) = \begin{cases} \emptyset \text{ if } c \geq 1 \\ (0,\infty) \text{ if } 0 \leq c < 1 \\ \mathbb{R} \text{ if } c < 0 \end{cases}\] These are all open sets, so \(f\) is lower semi-continuous. Now, changing the function: \[g(x) = \begin{cases} 0 \text{ if } x<0 \\ 1 \text{ if } x \geq 0 \end{cases}\] Checking upper semi-continuity: \[g^{-1}((-\infty, c)) = \{x\in \mathbb{R}: f(x) < c\}\] \[g^{-1}((-\infty, c)) = \begin{cases} \emptyset \text{ if } c \leq 0 \\ (\infty, 0) \text{ if } 0 < c \leq 1 \\ \mathbb{R} \text{ if } c > 1 \end{cases}\] Checking lower semi-continuity: \[g^{-1}((1/2, \infty)) = [0,\infty)\] which is NOT open. 7.1.2 Prop 4 “cont iff upper and lower semi-cont” A function \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is continuous if and only if it is both upper and lower semi-continuous. 7.1.2.1 Class 6 - 2023-08-21 7.1.3 Def “uniformly continuous” A function \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is uniformly continuous if, for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that for every \(x\in X\) with \(d_x(x, x') < \delta\) implies \(d_y(f(x), f(x')) < \varepsilon\). Graphical intuition: “if the function breaches top and bottom of box, it’s not uniformly cont” (\(\varepsilon\) is the vertical, \(\delta\) is the horizontal) 7.1.4 Def “Lipschitz Continuity” A funtion, \(f: X \rightarrow Y\) is Lipschitz Continuous if there exists \(K \in \mathbb{R}\) such that for any \(x,x' \in X\) \[d_y(f(x),f(x')) \leq K d_x(x,x')\] Graphical intuition: “can’t have any part of the function in the white cones” Example 5 Let \(f: \mathbb{R}^+ \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, f(x) = x^2\). Want to show: \(f\) is not uniformly continuous. To see this, fix \(\varepsilon > 0\) and fix \(\delta >0\). To show: there exists \(x, y \in \mathbb{R}^+\) with \(|x-y| < \delta\) but \(|x^2 - y^2| \geq \varepsilon\). Pick \(x = \varepsilon / \delta, y = x + \delta/2\). Then \(|x-y| = \delta/2 < \delta\) and \(|x^2 - y^2| = |(\varepsilon / \delta + \delta / 2)^2 - (\varepsilon/\delta)| = 2(\varepsilon/\delta)(\delta/2) + (\delta/2)^2 = \varepsilon + (\delta/2)^2 > \varepsilon\). So, \(f\) is not uniformly continuous. But, it might be continuous. Check that next. Showing \(f\) is continuous: Fix \(x\) and fix \(\varepsilon > 0\). Let \(\delta = \min\{1, \frac{\varepsilon}{2(x+1)}\}\). Fix \(y\) such that \[|x-y| < \delta \leq 1 \Rightarrow y < x+1 \Rightarrow |x^2 - y^2| = \\ |(x+y)(x-y)| = (x+y)|x-y| < 2(x+1)|x-y| < 2(x+1)\delta \leq \varepsilon\] *Note that \(y < x+1 \iff x+y < 2x+1 \iff x+y < 2(x+1)\) 7.1.4.1 Remember: uniform continuity \(\Rightarrow\) continuity. 7.1.5 Thm: “Intermediate Value Theorem” Let \(f: [\underline{x}, \bar{x}] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function. For each \(y^* \in \{y \in \mathbb{R}: f(\bar{x}) \geq y \geq f(\underline{x}) \text{ or } f(\underline{x}) \geq y \geq f(\bar{x})\}\) there exists \(x^* \in [\underline{x}, \bar{x}]\) such that \(f(x^*) = y^*\). 7.1.6 Def “maximum” “minimum” Say \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) achieves a maximum if there exists \(\bar{x} \in X\) such that \(f(\bar{x}) \geq f(x)\) for all \(x \in X\). Say \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) achieves a minimum if there exists \(\underline{x} \in X\) such that \(f(\underline{x}) \leq f(x)\) for all \(x \in X\). 7.1.7 Thm “Weierstrauss if compact then cont fn has max/min” Let \((X, d)\) be a compact metric space. Any continuous function \(f: X\rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) achieves a maximum and a minimum. So \((f_n: n\geq 1)\) does not uniformly converge to \(f\) (when the domain is all of \(\mathbb{R}_+\)) Given a function \(f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) and some nonempty \(E \subseteq X\), write \(f_{|E}\) for a function \(g: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) with \(g(x)=f(x)\) for all \(x\in E\). Call this “the restriction of \(f\) to \(E\). 8.0.3 Prop 1 “restricting domain doesn’t change uniform convergence” Let \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) be a sequence of functions in \(F(X, \mathbb{R})\) that converges uniformly to \(f \in F(X, \mathbb{R})\). For each non-empty \(E \subseteq X\), \((f_n|E: n \geq 1)\) is a sequence of functions in \(F(E,\mathbb{R})\) that converges uniformly to \(f_{|E} \in F(E, \mathbb{R})\). 8.0.3.1 Remember: increasing the domain can screw up uniform convergence. 8.0.4 Prop 2 “if fn is cont then f is cont” Let \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) be a sequence of functions in \(F(X, \mathbb{R})\). if, for each \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous at \(x^*\) then \(f\) is continuous at \(x^*\) if, for each \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous then \(f\) is continuous 8.0.5 Prop 3 “if seq is uni cont and uni conv then each fn converges to f(x)” Let \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) be a sequence on \(F(X, \mathbb{R})\) where each \(f_n\) is uniformly continuous and \((f_n: n\geq1)\) converges uniformly to \(f \in F(X, \mathbb{R})\). If \((x_n: n\geq1)\) is a sequence on \(X\) that converges to \(x \in X\), then \((f_n(x_n): n \geq 1)\) converges to \(f(x)\). Reminder: “Uniform metric”: \(d\) on \(F^B(X, \mathbb{R})\): \[d(f,g) = \sup_{x\in X} |f(x)-g(x)|\] Convergence under the uniform metric: Fix a seq \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) on \((F^B(X, \mathbb{R}),d)\). Then \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) converges to \(f\) if for each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists some \(N\) such that \(d(f_n, g) = \sup|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \varepsilon\) for all \(n \geq N\) *Note that \(|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \varepsilon\) is the same here as it was in the definition of uniform convergence. 8.0.6 Prop 4 “converges uniformly \(\iff\) converges under uniform metric” Fix a seq of functions \((f_n: n \geq 1)\) on \(F^B(x, \mathbb{R})\) the sequence converges uniformly to \(f \in F^B(X, \mathbb{R})\) if and only if it converges to \(f\) under the uniform metric.

10 Matrix Algebra 10.0.0.1 Operations of Matrices: matrix addition: \(A, B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m} \Rightarrow A + B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}\) where the \((ij)\)-entry is \(a_{ij} + b_{ij}\) scalar multiplication: \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}, \lambda \in \mathbb{R} \Rightarrow (ij)\)-entries are \(\lambda a_{ij}\) transpose: If \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m} \Rightarrow A^T \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}\) where (ij)-entry is \(a_{ji}\) 10.0.1 Def “Properties of transpose” \((A^T)^T = A\) \((A+B)^T = A^T+B^T\) \((\lambda A)^T = \lambda A^T\) \(A\) is symmetric \(\iff A = A^T\) 10.0.2 Prop 1 “matrix multiplication” \((AB)C=A(BC)\) \(A(B+C) = AB +AC\) \(\lambda (AB) = (\lambda A)B = A(\lambda B)\) \((AB)^T = B^TA^T\) 10.0.3 Remark \(AA^T\) is symmetric 10.0.4 Def “orthogonal” The square matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) is orthogonal if \(AA^T = Id^n\). 10.0.4.1 Remember: if orthogonal then invertible 10.0.5 Prop 2 “orthonogal iff” A matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}\) is orthogonal if and only if both of the following hold: For each \(i \neq j, \vec{A}[i,*] \cdot \vec{A}[j, *] = 0\) and \(\vec{A}[*,i] \cdot \vec{A}[*,j] = 0\) for each \(i, ||\vec{A}[i,*]||=1\) and \(||\vec{A}[*,j]||=1\). 10.0.6 Def “invertible matrix” Fix a square matrix \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\). Call \(A\) invertible if there exists another matrix \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\) such that \[AB = BA = Id^n.\] 10.0.7 Prop 3 “inverse is unique” If \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\) is invertible, there exists a unique \[B\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\] such that \(AB=BA=Id^n\). Proof suppose \(AB=BA = Id^n\) and \(AC=CA = Id^n\). Then it suffices to show \(B=C\). Recall that \(C = C Id^n\). Then \[C = C Id^n = C(AB) = (CA)B = Id^nB = B.\] 10.0.7.1 Remember: The inverse of an orthogonal \(A\) is \(A^T\) 10.0.8 Prop 4 “properties of invertibility” Let \(A, B\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\) invertible matrices. \((A^{-1})^{-1}=A\) \((A^T)^{-1}=(A^{-1})^T\) \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\) if \(\lambda \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}, (\lambda A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda} A^{-1}\) 10.0.9 Prop 5 “invertible iff rows, cols lin ind” Fix \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\). The following are equivalent: A is invertible \(\{\vec{A}[1,*], ..., \vec{A}[n, *]\}\) (aka all rows) are linearly independent \(\{\vec{A}[*,1], ..., \vec{A}[*, n]\}\) (aka all columns) are linearly independent 10.0.10 Def “determinant” Defined inductively. n=1: \[A = [a_{11}]; det(A) = a_{11}\] Assume we have defined \(det(B)\) for each \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\). Fix a matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times (n +1)}\) for \(i,j = 1, ..., n+1\). For each \(i, j = 1, ..., n\) construct a new matrix by deleting row \(i\), column \(j\). \(M_{ij}\) is the matrix obtained by deleting row \(i\), column \(j\). \(\det(M_{ij})\) is called a “minor” \(det(M_{ij})\) was defined already. Set \[c_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j}det(M_{ij})\] Then \[\det(A) = \sum_{j=1}^n a_{ij}c_{ij}, \text{ for some } i.\] Note \(c_{ij}\) is called the \((ij)\)-cofactor of \(A\). 10.0.11 Prop 6 “determinants and invertibility” Fix \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\). if \(\det(A) = 0\) then \(A\) is not invertible if \(\det(A) \neq 0\) then \(A\) is invertible. Moreover, \(A^{-1} = B\) where \[b_{ij} = \frac{(-1)^{i+j}\det(M_{ji})}{\det(A)} = \frac{c_{ji}}{\det(A)}\] 10.0.11.1 Class 9 - (2023-08-24) 10.0.12 Prop 7 “properties of determinants” Fix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}\) the determinant is linear in row/column vectors if \(\vec{A}[i,*] = \vec{x} + \vec{y}\) then \[\det \bigg( \vec{A}[1,\ast] ... \vec{A}[i,\ast] ... \vec{A}[n,\ast]\bigg) = \bigg( \vec{A}[1,\ast] ... \vec{x} ... \vec{A}[n,\ast]\bigg) = \bigg( \vec{A}[1,\ast] ... \vec{y} ... \vec{A}[n,\ast]\bigg)\] if \(\lambda \in \mathbb{R}\) then \[\det \bigg( \vec{A}[1,\ast] ... \lambda\vec{A}[i,\ast] ... \vec{A}[n,\ast]\bigg) = \lambda\det \bigg( \vec{A}[1,\ast] ... \vec{A}[i,\ast] ... \vec{A}[n,\ast]\bigg)\] the rows are linearly dependent iff an only if \(\det(A)=0\) if \(A = Id^n, \det(A) = 1\) \(\det(A) = \det(A^T)\) \(\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)\) 10.0.12.1 Term “rank” Row-rank of A: \(dim(span(\{\vec{A}[1, \ast], ... ,\vec{A}[n, \ast]\})\) Col-rank of A: \(dim(span(\{\vec{A}[\ast, 1], ... ,\vec{A}[\ast, n]\})\) Result: row-rank \(A\) = col-rank \(A\) = \(rank(A)\) 10.0.12.2 Term “trace” Trace of \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\) is \(\sum_{i=1}^n{a_{ii}}\) (sum of diagonals)

abla g_i(x^*) \cdot \vec{x} < 0\). Examples 7 and 8 fail Mangasarian-Fromovitz! In 8, there was no \(\vec{x}\) with * \((0,1) \cdot \vec{x} < 0\) or * \((0,-1) \cdot \vec{x} < 0\) In general, we’ll have a problem satisfying MF if \(i,j\) are binding at \(x^*\) with \[
abla g_i(x^*) = - 
abla g_j(x^*)\] 13.3.2 Def “Slater condition” Say the max inequality problem satisfies the Slater condition if there exists \(x \in X\) such that for each \(i = 1, ..., m, g_i(X) < 0\). 13.3.3 Prop 7 “Slater iff MF at each feasible” Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) be open, \(f\) continuously differentiable and for each \(i, g_i\) is convex and continuously differentiable. Then the Slater condition holds if and only if Mangasarian-Fromovitz holds at each feasible \(x^*\).

OR \[f(x,y) = x^n \phi(\frac{y}{n})\] 14.0.2 Thm “Euler’s thm” If \(f\) is a (partial) function of \(n\) real variables that is positively homogeneous of degree \(k\), and continuously differentiable in some open subset of \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\) then it satisfies in this open set the partial differential equation \[{k f(x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}{\frac {\partial f}{\partial x_{i}}}(x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n}).}\] 14.0.3 Def “Lagrange multiplier”
